content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95E
Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Inter
(916) 322-5660 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

November 27, 1979

MR. Quirino Paris
528 George Town Place
Davis, CA 95616

A-779-133

Dear Mr. Paris:

Thank you for your letter dated October 31, 1979, asking
whether it would be a conflict of interest or other illegal
act for the City of Davis to accept a $23,000 grant from
Pacific Gas & Electric Company. This office interprets only
the Political Reform Act of 1974, Government Code Sections
81000-91014. To find out what other laws, if any, may be
applicable I suggest you contact a private attorney or your
local District Attorney.

The conflict of interest prohibition in the Political
Reform Act, Government Code Section 87100, provides as follows:

No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

The Political Reform Act, therefore, requires public
officials, as individuals, to refrain from participating in
those decisions that may affect their personal financial
interests. The Act does not prohibit the City Council as a
governmental entity from accepting grants from business
entities who may have an interest in the city's activity.
Therefore, it does not constitute a conflict of interest for
the City of Davis to accept a $23,000 grant from Pacific Gas
& Electric Company.

Sincerely,

Dwight F. Dickerson
Counsel
Legal Division

DED:kp

Certified with return receipt.

October 31, 1979

The California Fair Political
Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

On October 9, 1979 PG&E (Pacific Gas and Electricity) has offered
and on October 11, 1979 the Davis City Council has accepted a grant
of $ 23,000 intended for financing an energy aide.

Two facts are important.

1. The Davis City Council has been considering the adoption of
a highly controversial ordinance to retrofit houses for energy con-
servation on a mandatory basis.

2. In February, 1979, the Davis City Council established the Ad
Hoc Energy Generation Committee which was instructed to identifying
"alternative methods of generating electricity ... for distribution
in Davis on a more cost effective basis than the utility company
currently serving Davis."

Therefore, the grant by PG&E seems clearly designed to directly
influence the formulation of City policies in the area of energy.

In view of the two important facts described above, I ask the Commission to determine whether the acceptance by the Davis City Council
of PG&E's grant constitutes a conflict of interest or any other
improper act.

If the Commission is not empowered to judge the actions of City
Councils, what is the appropriate supervisory agency I should contact?

Sincerely,

Quirino Paris
528 Georgetown Place
Davis, CA 95616

sections.question: Whether the acceptance by the Davis City Council of PG&E's grant constitutes a conflict of interest or any other improper act. If the Commission is not empowered to judge the actions of City Councils, what is the appropriate supervisory agency to contact?
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether the Davis City Council's acceptance of a $23,000 grant from PG&E for an energy aide constitutes a conflict of interest, given that the Council was simultaneously considering mandatory energy conservation ordinances and had established a committee to explore alternative electricity generation methods. The requester also asks what agency to contact if the FPPC lacks jurisdiction over city councils.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document contains no CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section. Letter is dated October 31, 1979 and signed by 'Paris' from Davis, CA. The FPPC address and certified mail notation at top indicate this was sent to FPPC. No formal QUESTION section header, but the substantive question is clearly articulated in the body of the letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the acceptance by the Davis City Council of PG&E's grant constitutes a conflict of interest or any other improper act. If the Commission is not empowered to judge the actions of City Councils, what is the appropriate supervisory agency to contact?

embedding.summary: A Davis resident submits an inquiry to the FPPC requesting determination of whether the Davis City Council's acceptance of a $23,000 grant from PG&E constitutes a conflict of interest, noting the timing coincides with the Council's consideration of energy-related policy matters.
